all_candidates.title: Tour de France 2017: Stage 9 Recap | NBC Sports

transcript.clean_text: Every tour to front stage win deserves celebration, especially when it's your first and France is your native country. 24y old Lillian Kjan overcoming cramps at the three mile mark, showing lots of mental and physical toughness, enjoying the spoils of victory. He also won the polka dot jersey as the king of the mountains today. That's your stage winner. How about the overall leader, Chris Fume, cooling down after a tough ride himself. He will stay in yellow. He is the overall leader once again. Thanks for coming back with us. Paul Burmeister, Bob Ro and also Christian Vandervel. Tough day for all the riders. Describe what Lilian and Kajjan did to win. The the thing he did that uh is the hallmark of great cyclists is resist. Having the ability, the wherewithal, the mental toughness as his body was disintegrating to resist the riders that were chasing him to the finish line. We're going to be seeing a lot of Lilian Kjan in the future. That's a great ride in this year's tournament. What happened on that last climb, guys? It was absolute chaos. Nicholas Roach lighting it up. He was in the second group. He bridged up to the front group. He went past the front group, dropping most of the riders from the early morning. But this is the emphatic and racewinning attack that no one could follow by Lilian Kmean. And he would ride that momentum all the way to the finish. And these are the riders he had to overcome. Robert Kasync a great climber. Serge Powell's Nico Ro Kan streaking to the line in spite of his body cramping up in spite of fans getting in his way, getting in his head, but kept looking back because Hayink was never that much more than 10, 11, 12, 15 seconds behind him the entire last climb. and to have the wherewithal, the patience, the maturity to not panic, stay within himself in spite of his body cramping in the finishing straightaway almost. At this point, he's thinking, I'm going to win. Then all of a sudden, it looked like he was absolutely glued to the road and couldn't go any further. and he somehow he found the ability to overcome that and regain his momentum stretching out a little bit. How bad is that? How bad is that when that happens? I it's hard to put into words how horrible that is and especially if you put into the situation where Lillian's in where he's four kilometers from the finish line first win of his career at the tour to France and you think that you can't pedal another pedal stroke and you come so far but I mean yeah of course the pain is is something but more of the emotional pain and struggle that you're trying to just overcome that like Bob said earlier and then that shows a lot though not to panic relax a little bit you still have 40 seconds in your pocket over a charge and Robert has sink He obviously made it though with plenty of time to spare. Great victory salute as you'd always want to come across the line. If if you had all the dreams come true since you're 10 years old, think about what you're going to do when you come across the line for the first your first win at the Tour to France. That would be it. Especially the stage today, Paul. I mean, come on. They went from the gun flat out. Four and a half hours of racing, tons of climbing. There was never one time where you could truly relax. Most of the Pelon remember this stage for many many years. Lillian Kan lots of hugs from his team and then smiles to the crowd as we mentioned right at the top. He's from France making the victory all the more sweet. Continue to enjoy his effort today. His winning effort. The stage eight results. Richie Port there perhaps Chris Froom's top challenger here. That's what he did. And Chris Froom also 50 seconds back. And we've seen Call Me John celebrate a number of ways. Here he is at top stage winning podium for the first time in his career. Christian, you said that the field, especially this man, will be remembering the stage for a long time. I think tomorrow morning they'll have their first their first chance to really remember it when we approach the hardest stage in the tour to France. We'll see if Lillian Kan has anything left for tomorrow. It wouldn't be surprising to me if he lost a big chunk of time tomorrow, but it will all be worth it because of this win today. That's so true, especially you think in years past when you have the first big mountain stage. Usually the day before, everyone takes it pretty gingerely and that was not the case today. Before the race, guys, we talked about one of the main questions heading in. Would it be an opportunity for the GC contenders to put some time into Chris Froom as we take a look at the winner Lily and call me John shaking hands, enjoying the win? Bob, what do you see in the yellow jersey standings as Chris Froom found a way to stay up top? Team's guy was fantastic in the finale. They had put two guys in the breakaway. I I found myself a bit further back and then yeah, just had momentum, so I thought, why not? I I said to Richie with 10k to go. Is Richie is Nico gonna still is up for the stage? Because I didn't have a clue what was going on in front. But yeah, it's a brutal brutal day which is what we expected. But wow, like we rode like so fast all day, you know. Any concern with making an effort like that knowing tomorrow's an even tougher day? Everybody everybody did the same effort, you know. It was kind of like I didn't all I did was a little sprint. That's really just the feather in his cap at all times. But Dan's looking fantastic. He was my pick actually for today. He's probably the fastest out of all the climbers and all the GC guys. So, whenever Dan sees an opportunity, he doesn't think he just does it. And that makes Dan very special. I think it might be Christian, correct me if I'm wrong, the calmst person in the entire professional pelathon. It looked like he had bottles on his back and in his stomach. So, he might have been coming back from the team car with his hands full trying to give bottles back to his teammates. Good looking shot of the Jura Mountain. Stage eight took place right here. Let's see what Chris Froom had to say afterward. Chris, that was extremely intense from kilometer zero all the way to the finish. Were you expecting that? Um, honestly, no. Not not quite as intense as it was today. That was that was a really tough day, especially with tomorrow in mind. Um, yeah, that that was that was really quite quite selective. Um, I think my teammates teammates were fantastic today. They from start to finish were in control of the race. And I mean the pressure was on. We had a lot of guys up front who who were up there on GC and were were a real threat. So um yeah, team team really had to sort of dig out pull out all the stops today and uh the guys the guys were fantastic. What did did you talk about about this morning during a team briefing? The goal was to control the situation and have as many of your teammates at the front of the pack. Yeah, I mean um it was tactically quite a quite an interesting stage. I mean we we we went with the plan of putting putting Sergio and Mikuel Lander in the break. Um seeing as they're also up there on GC, but at the same time there was Matias Frank up there. Uh at one stage it was Luv also up there. Um also threats on GC. So it was a difficult one. Even though we had two guys in the break, we couldn't really give them too much room. So, um, tough day in the end and tomorrow, the the big one, the the stage that many considers maybe the the hardest of the tool front. It's going to be a fantastic battle. I think so. Especially after today, I think tomorrow is going to be a really really decisive stage of this year's tour and we could see I think um certainly from from the main GC guys, I think those gaps are going to open right up tomorrow. So, um, yeah, I I think all in all, we're going to see a very aggressive day of racing tomorrow.
